text.title: python-web-frameworks Failure Modes
text.summary: Five recurring antipatterns when picking and operating Django/FastAPI/Flask.

antipattern.description: Mixing framework idioms across the codebase: `@app.route` decorators inside a FastAPI module, `User.objects.filter()` inside a non-Django process, Flask-Login middleware in a FastAPI app, or `flask.request` imports next to `Depends(...)`.
antipattern.reason: Each framework defines its own routing, ORM access, session/auth, and middleware contract. Cross-pollination fails silently (Flask's `request` proxy resolves in FastAPI but is empty) or at runtime (`User.objects` requires `django.setup()` and a configured `DJANGO_SETTINGS_MODULE`). The decision-record ADR makes this detectable in review.

antipattern.description: Using `@app.on_event("startup")` / `@app.on_event("shutdown")` in FastAPI projects on FastAPI ≥0.93.
antipattern.reason: The `on_event` decorators were deprecated in FastAPI 0.93 in favour of the `lifespan` context manager passed to `FastAPI(lifespan=...)`. `on_event` is still accepted but emits a `DeprecationWarning` and may be removed. New code must use `lifespan`.

antipattern.description: Quoting RPS / throughput numbers ("FastAPI does 3,000 RPS", "Django serves 1,500 RPS") as authoritative without a named benchmark and a workload description.
antipattern.reason: Web framework RPS depends on the workload (sync vs async I/O, payload size, DB round trips), the worker model (gunicorn sync, uvicorn workers, ASGI mode), and the hardware. Quoting general numbers in an ADR is a maturity smell. Run a representative load test before pinning a perf claim.

antipattern.description: Starting with Flask "for simplicity", then accumulating Flask-RESTX + Flask-Migrate + Flask-Login + Flask-WTF + Flask-Admin until the dependency list rivals Django's batteries.
antipattern.reason: At that point Flask adds friction (manual wiring of extensions, no unified migrations story) without delivering features Django already includes. The decision matrix should have selected Django upfront. Use this antipattern as the trigger for re-platforming.

antipattern.description: Writing `async def` views in Flask and expecting concurrent request handling.
antipattern.reason: WSGI is synchronous. Flask's `async` view support wraps the coroutine in a sync call via `asgiref.sync.async_to_sync` — there is zero concurrency benefit, only added cognitive overhead. For real async, switch to FastAPI/Starlette or Quart.
